questions.question: RNNs maintain state by:
options: Passing a hidden state vector h(t) from the previous timestep
questions.explanation: h(t) = tanh(Wh·h(t-1) + Wx·x(t)). This hidden state is the 'memory' of the RNN.

questions.question: The vanishing gradient problem in vanilla RNNs occurs because:
options: Gradients shrink exponentially as they backpropagate through many timesteps
The learning rate is too high
The hidden state is too large
questions.explanation: BPTT through long sequences multiplies gradients repeatedly by small values — early timesteps get near-zero gradients.

questions.question: LSTM's forget gate controls:
options: How much of the previous cell state to keep or discard
questions.explanation: forget gate = sigmoid(Wf·[h(t-1),x(t)]). Near 0 = forget, near 1 = keep. Trained to learn what to remember.

questions.question: GRU differs from LSTM by:
options: Having 2 gates instead of 3 — simpler, often comparable performance
questions.explanation: GRU has reset and update gates. ~33% fewer parameters than LSTM. Preferred when speed matters.

questions.question: Teacher forcing during RNN training means:
options: Using the ground-truth previous output as input during training
questions.explanation: Teacher forcing: feed the true target y(t-1) as input at step t during training, even if the model's prediction was wrong.

questions.question: Bidirectional RNNs process sequences:
options: Both forward and backward, combining both hidden states
questions.explanation: BiRNN runs two RNNs (forward + backward). At each step, h = [h_forward; h_backward] captures past AND future context.

questions.question: Time series cross-validation must:
options: Preserve temporal order — train on past, test on future only
questions.explanation: Using future data to train on past = data leakage. Time-series CV uses expanding or rolling windows.

questions.question: The 'sequence-to-sequence' architecture is used for:
options: Translation, summarisation — encoder reads input, decoder generates output
questions.explanation: Seq2Seq: encoder encodes the full input sequence to a context vector; decoder generates the output sequence.

questions.question: MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) is preferred for forecasting because:
options: It's scale-free — 5% error means the same regardless of absolute values
questions.explanation: MAPE = mean(|actual-pred|/|actual|)*100. Allows comparison across series with different magnitudes.

questions.question: Stationary time series means:
options: Mean and variance don't change over time — required for ARIMA
questions.explanation: Non-stationarity (trend, changing variance) violates ARIMA assumptions. Use differencing or transformation first.
